subject: Mathematics
q: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇔</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></math> is:
question: The statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>⇔</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></math> is:
solution:
~p⇔~q∧q  =p⇔q∧q

Proof: Solving the given ~p⇔~q

=~p→~q∧~q→p

=~~p∨~q∧q∨p

=~~p∧q∧p∨q

=p∧q∨~p∨q      ...1

Now, p⇔q≡p→q∧q→p

=~p∨q∧~q∨p

=~p∨q∧~q∨~p∨q∧p

=~p∧~q∨q∧~q∨~p∧p∨q∧p

=~p∧~q∨c∨c∨q∧p where c is contradiction

=~p∧~q∨p∧q

=~p∨q∨p∧q        ...2

So from equation 1 & 2 we get

~p⇔~q∧q≡p⇔q∧q

p q p↔q p↔q∧q p→q p→q∧q p→1∧p
T T T T T T T
T F F F F F F
F T F F T T F
F F T F T F F

∴  ~p⇔~q∧q is equivalent to p⇒q∧p.

subject: Mathematics
q: Negation of the Boolean statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to:
question: Negation of the Boolean statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to:
solution:
Given,

p∨q⇒~r∨p

≡~p∨q∨~r∨p

≡~p∧~q∨p∨~r

≡~p∨p∧~q∨p∨~r

≡~q∨p∨~r

So negation of~q∨p∨~r  is ~p∧q∧r.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement-1: The statement $A \rightarrow(B \rightarrow A)$ is equivalent to $A \rightarrow(A \vee B)$.

Statement-2: The statement $\sim[(\mathrm{A} \wedge \mathrm{B}) \rightarrow$ $(\sim \mathrm{A} \vee \mathrm{B})]$ is a Tautology.
question:
Statement-1: The statement $A \rightarrow(B \rightarrow A)$ is equivalent to $A \rightarrow(A \vee B)$.

Statement-2: The statement $\sim[(\mathrm{A} \wedge \mathrm{B}) \rightarrow$ $(\sim \mathrm{A} \vee \mathrm{B})]$ is a Tautology.
options: Statement- 1 is false; Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true; Statement-2 is true; Statement- 2 is not correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true; Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true; Statement-2 is true; Statement- 2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $(\mathrm{p} \wedge \sim \mathrm{q}) \wedge(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{r}) \rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then the truth values of $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}$ and $\mathrm{r}$ are respectively
question: If $(\mathrm{p} \wedge \sim \mathrm{q}) \wedge(\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{r}) \rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then the truth values of $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}$ and $\mathrm{r}$ are respectively
solution: As the truth table for the $(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r)$ $\rightarrow \sim p \vee q$ is false, then only possible values of $(p, q, r)$ is $(T, F, T)$ $\begin{array}{llllll}p & q & r & \sim q & p \wedge \sim q & p \wedge r \\ \sim p & \sim p \vee q & (p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r)(p \wedge \sim q) \wedge(p \wedge r) \\ \rightarrow \sim p \vee q\end{array}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Negation of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is :
question: Negation of the statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is :
solution:
The negation of the statement (p∨r)⇒(q∨r) is given by

~p∨r⇒q∨r

≡~~p∨r∨q∨r

≡p∨r∧~q∨r

≡p∨r∧~q∧~r

≡p∨r∧~r∧~q

≡p∧~r∨r∧~r∧~q

≡((p∧~r)∨ϕ)∧(~q)

≡(p∧~r)∧(~q)

≡p∧~q∧~r

q: The number of choices for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⇔</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology, is
question: The number of choices for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⇒</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⇔</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>, such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mi>Δ</mi><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>Δ</mi><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology, is

Now for pΔ~q∨~pΔq to be true, Δ should be ∨ or ⇒

As p∨~q∨~p∨q≡t; p⇒~q∨~p⇒q≡t

q: The contrapositive of the statement "I go to school if it does not rain" is
question: The contrapositive of the statement "I go to school if it does not rain" is
options: If it rains, I go to school.
If it rains, I do not go to school.
If I go to school, it rains.
If I do not go to school, it rains.
solution:
Let p= It does not rain

q= I go to school

Contrapositive of if p  → q is if ∼q → ∼p

So, If it rains, I do not go to school.

q: The contrapositive of the statement "If I reach the station in time, then I will catch the train" is
question: The contrapositive of the statement "If I reach the station in time, then I will catch the train" is
options: If I do not reach the station in time, then I will catch the train.
If do not reach the station in time, then I will not catch the train.
If I will catch the train, then I reach the station in time.
If I will not catch the train, then I do not reach the station in time.
solution:
Contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

⇒The statement will be "If I will not catch the train, then I do not reach the station in time".

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider :
Statement - I : p∧~q∧~p∧q is a fallacy.
Statement - II : p→q↔~q→~p is a tautology.
question:
Consider :
Statement - I : p∧~q∧~p∧q is a fallacy.
Statement - II : p→q↔~q→~p is a tautology.
options: Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> true; Statement -<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true; Statement -<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is a correct explanation for Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math>.

Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true; Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true; Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is not a correct explanation for Statement - <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math>.
solution:
The truth table for Statement I is

p q ~p ~q p∧~q ~p∧q p∧~q∧~p∧q

TTFF

 TFTF FFTT FTFT FTFF FFTF

FFFF

Hence, it is a fallacy. 

The truth table for statement II is

p q ~p ~q p→q ~q→~p p→q↔~q→~p
TTFF TFTF FFTT FTFT TFTT TFTT TTTT

Hence, it is a tautology.

But, the second statement is not a correct explanation for the first.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Among the statements

S1: p⇒q∨~p∧q is a tautology

S2: q⇒p⇒~p∧q is a contradiction
question:
Among the statements

S1: p⇒q∨~p∧q is a tautology

S2: q⇒p⇒~p∧q is a contradiction
solution:
Plotting the Truth table of the given expression we get,

p q ~p ~p∧q p⇒q q⇒p p⇒q∨~p∧q q⇒p∨~p∧q
T T F F T T T T
T F F F F T F T
F T T T T F T T
F F T F T T T T

Hence, q⇒p∨~p∧q is a tautology, and p⇒q∨~p∧q is neither tautology and contradiction,

So both statements are false.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the statement: "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>n</mi></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>41</mn></math> is prime". Then which one of the following is true?
question: Consider the statement: "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>n</mi></mfenced><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>41</mn></math> is prime". Then which one of the following is true?
solution:
Given Pn=n2-n+41

P3=9-3+41=47

P5=25-5+41=61

Hence P3 and P5 both are prime.

Hence, both are true.

q: Negation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is

Expression p∧q∧~p∧q

~p∧q∧~p∧q

Now using demorgan's law ~A∧B≡~A∨~B & ~A∨B≡~A∧~B we get,

~p∧q∧~p∧q

≡~p∧q∧~p∨~q

≡~p∧q∧~p∨q∧~q

≡~p∧q∧~p∨F

≡~p∧q∧~p

≡~p∨~q∧~p

≡~p∨~q∨p

≡~p∨~q∨p

≡~p∧q∨p

q: The contrapositive of the statement "If it is raining, then I will not come", is
question: The contrapositive of the statement "If it is raining, then I will not come", is
options: if I will come, then it is not raining.
if I will come, then it is raining.
if I will not come, then it is raining.
if I will not come, then it is not raining.
solution:
Contrapositive of p⇒q is ~q⇒~ p.

So contrapositive of the statement "If it is raining, then I will not come", would be, 
"If I will come, then it is not raining".

subject: Mathematics
q: The Statement that is TRUE among the following is
question: The Statement that is TRUE among the following is
options: The contrapositive of $3 x+2=8 \Rightarrow x=2$ is $x \neq 2$ $\Rightarrow 3 x+2 \neq 8$.
The converse of $\tan \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ is $x \neq 0 \Rightarrow$ $\tan x=0$.
$p \vee q$ and $p \wedge q$ have the same truth table.
solution:
Only statement given in option
(a) is true.
(b) The converse of $\tan \mathrm{x}=0 \Rightarrow x=0$ is $x=0 \Rightarrow \tan x=0$
$\therefore$ Statement (b) is false
(c) $\sim(p \Rightarrow q$ is dquivalent to $p \wedge \sim q$
$\therefore$ Statement given in option (c) is false.
(d) No, $p \vee q$ and $p \wedge q$ does not have the same truth value.

q: The expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is logically equivalent to
question: The expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is logically equivalent to
solution:
We know that ~p→q=p∧~q.

Hence, the expression ~~p→q is logically equivalent to ~p∧~q.

q: The contrapositive of the statement ''if I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor" is
question: The contrapositive of the statement ''if I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor" is
options: if I will go to the doctor, then I am not feeling well.
if I am feeling well, then I will not go to the doctor.
if I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
if I will go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.
solution:
Statement: If I am not feeling well, then I will go to the doctor.

Let p: I am not feeling well and q: I will go to the doctor.

The given statement is p→q.

We know that the contrapositive of p→q is ~q→~p.

Contrapositive: If I will not go to the doctor, then I am feeling well.

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></math> is false, then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></math> is false, then the truth values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively
solution:
Recall p⇒q≡F  if and only if T⇒F≡F

So, p∧~q∧p∧r→~p∨q

Taking Option A : T∧F∧T∧T→F∨T

≡F∧T→T

≡F→T≡T

Hence, option A is false.

Taking Option B : F∧F∧F∧F→T∨T

≡F→T≡T

Hence, option B is false.

Taking Option C : T∧T∧T∧T→F∨F

≡T→F≡F

Hence, option C is correct.

Taking Option D : F∧T∧F∧F→T∨F

≡F∧F→T

≡F→T≡T

Hence, option D is false.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>△</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∇</mo><mo>∈</mo><mo>{</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>}</mo></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>△</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a tautology. Then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>△</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∇</mo><mo>∈</mo><mo>{</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo><mo>}</mo></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>△</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∇</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a tautology. Then
solution:
Given,

△,∇∈{∧,∨} be such that (p→q)△(p∇q) is a tautology

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\mathrm{q}$ is false and $\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{r}$ is true, then which one of the following statements is a tautology?
question: If $\mathrm{q}$ is false and $\mathrm{p} \wedge \mathrm{q} \leftrightarrow \mathrm{r}$ is true, then which one of the following statements is a tautology?
solution:
$q$ is false and $[(p \wedge q) \leftrightarrow r]$ is true As $(p \wedge q)$ is false

$[$ False $\leftrightarrow r]$ is true

Hence $r$ is false

Option (1): says $p \vee r$,

Since $r$ is false

Hence $(p \vee r)$ can either be true or false

Option (2): says $(p \wedge r) \rightarrow(p \vee r)$

$(p \wedge r)$ is false

Since, $F \rightarrow T$ is true and

$F \rightarrow F$ is also true

Hence, it is a tautology

Option (3): $(p \vee r) \rightarrow(p \wedge r)$

i.e. $(p \vee r) \rightarrow F$

It can either be true or false

Option (4): $(p \wedge r)$

Since, $r$ is false

Hence, $(p \wedge r)$ is false.

solution:
Given,

(p∧(~q))∨((~p) ∧ q) ∨((~p) ∧ (~q))

=((~p) ∧ q) ∨((~p) ∧ (~q))∨(p∧(~q))

=~p∧q∨~q∨p∧~q

We know that ~A∨A=T, where T is tautology,

=~p∧T∨p∧~q

=~p∨p∧~q

=~p∨p∧~p∨~q

=T∧~p∨~q

=~p∨~q
